coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorometric and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform for rapid analyzing Cr(VI), Ag(I) and HCHO in food and environmental samples based on N-doped carbon nanodots and o-phenylenediamine
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Wenwen
Lei, Yaya
Yu, Xiaoxiao
Wu, Yiwei
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) were synthesized simply and efficiently using glutathione. The fluorescence emission of N-CNDs at 430 nm was effectively quenched by the fluorophore 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), produced through the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) under the catalysis of Cr(VI)/Ag(I). This quenching was attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect, while a new fluorescence emission at 560 nm was observed. Furthermore, the redox and chromogenic reaction of Cr(VI) and OPD at pH 5.4 could be effectively inhibited by formaldehyde (HCHO), resulting in the activation of N-CNDs fluorescence and the quenching of DAP fluorescence. Consequently, dual-signal sensing platforms for the rapid analysis of Cr(VI) and Ag(I) using N-CNDs/OPD and HCHO using N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) were successfully constructed. By incorporating a masking reagent such as H2O2 for Cr(VI) and Cl− for Ag(I), the established sensing platform exhibited excellent selectivity and practical applicability for detecting Cr(VI), Ag(I), and HCHO in food and environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cr(VI), Ag(Ⅰ) and HCHO
2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP)
N-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs)
o-phenylenediamine (OPD)
Ratiometric fluorometric and colorimetric dual-signal
Sensing platform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N-CNDs/OPD ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal detection of Ag(I) and Cr(VI)
N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal detection of HCHO
N-CNDs/OPD ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal detection of Ag(I) and Cr(VI) in real samples
N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal detection of HCHO in real samples
Synthesis route of N-CNDs and construction mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal Cr(VI), Ag(I) by N-CNDs/OPD and HCHO by N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI)
Optimization of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal Cr(VI) and Ag(I) sensing platform based on N-CNDs/OPD
Optimization of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal HCHO sensing platform based on N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI)
Effect of time and Cr(VI) concentration
Exploring the mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal probing Ag(I)/Cr(VI) by N-CNDs/OPD and HCHO by N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(Ⅵ)
Analytical performance of a new N-CNDs/OPD-based ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal method for the detection of Ag(I), Cr(VI) and HCHO sensing

Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a), XRD (b), Zeta potential (c) and FT-IR (d) images of N-CNDs.
XPS survey of N-CNDs and high resolution of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s.
Synthesis route of N-CNDs and construction mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal Cr(VI), Ag(I) and HCHO sensing platform based on N-CNDs and OPD.

Selectivity of N-CNDs/OPD sensing platform toward metal ions (a) and masking experiment of Ag(Ⅰ) (b) and Cr(Ⅵ) (c). N-CNDs: 8.13 mM; OPD: 10 mM; Metal ions:15 μg mL−1; Ag(Ⅰ): 60 μM; Cr(Ⅵ): 115 μM; KCl: 100 μM; H2O2: 1 mM.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the proportional fluorescence sensing platform in presence of different concentrations of Ag(I) (a), Cr(VI) (e) and HCHO (i) (Inset: color under 365 nm UV lamp); Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the concentrations of Ag(I) (b), Cr(VI) (f) and HCHO (j). UV absorption spectra of the colorimetric sensing platform in the presence of different concentrations of Ag(I) (c), Cr(VI) (g) and HCHO (k) (Inset: color in natural light); Linear relationship between the UV absorption peak and the concentrations of Ag(I) (d), Cr(VI) (h) and HCHO (l), respectively. N-CNDs: 8.13 mM; OPD: 10 mM.
Analytical results of Ag(I), Cr(Ⅵ) and HCHO in actual samples.

row.entry: Results for the determination of Ag(I) in actual samples
Results for the determination of Cr(Ⅵ) in actual samples
Results for the determination of HCHO in foods

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorometric and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform for rapid analyzing Cr(VI), Ag(I) and HCHO in food and environmental samples based on N-doped carbon nanodots and o-phenylenediamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry
Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesizing N-CNDs using GSH and construction of a new ratiometric fluorometric and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platforms for rapid analyzing Cr(VI), Ag(I) by N-CNDs/OPD and HCHO by N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI).

Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) were synthesized simply and efficiently using glutathione. The fluorescence emission of N-CNDs at 430 nm was effectively quenched by the fluorophore 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), produced through the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) under the catalysis of Cr(VI)/Ag(I). This quenching was attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect, while a new fluorescence emission at 560 nm was observed. Furthermore, the redox and chromogenic reaction of Cr(VI) and OPD at pH 5.4 could be effectively inhibited by formaldehyde (HCHO), resulting in the activation of N-CNDs fluorescence and the quenching of DAP fluorescence. Consequently, dual-signal sensing platforms for the rapid analysis of Cr(VI) and Ag(I) using N-CNDs/OPD and HCHO using N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) were successfully constructed. By incorporating a masking reagent such as H2O2 for Cr(VI) and Cl− for Ag(I), the established sensing platform exhibited excellent selectivity and practical applicability for detecting Cr(VI), Ag(I), and HCHO in food and environmental samples.

ce:keyword.ce:text: inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry/ mass spectrometry

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals, including Ag(I) and Cr(VI), present in soil, surface water, and groundwater, are introduced through chemical industrial effluents and subsequently enter the human body via food or water consumption. Accumulation of these heavy metals in the body can lead to acute or chronic poisoning (Zhu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017), resulting in various health issues such as cardiomegaly, growth retardation, skin damage, cognitive deficits, genotoxicity, and even cancer. Formaldehyde (HCHO), a ubiquitous compound in daily life, poses health risks when inhaled, causing symptoms like headaches, dizziness, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and potential impacts on blood pressure, liver function, and kidney function (Kundu et al., 2019). Hence, the development of a simple, sensitive, and rapid method for monitoring Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO in real samples is crucial for assessing ecological impacts and accurately diagnosing diseases.

Currently available methods for analyzing Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO include AAS (Dos et al., 2018), ICP-OES/MS (Spanu et al., 2021; Cang et al., 2022), HPLC (Saraiva et al., 2021), and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Zhou et al., 2017). These techniques offer high sensitivity and specificity but are limited in their application due to factors such as cost, operational complexity, lengthy analytical periods, as well as the complex matrices and low analyte concentrations often found in real samples. Recently, advancements in rapid analysis and detection technologies, such as SERS, have improved detection platforms enabling direct SERS spectra acquisition for analytes like nitrite, Pb2+, and Hg2+ (Yao et al., 2023). However, fluorescence-based methods remain a research hotspot due to their advantages of simplicity, high sensitivity, selectivity, and fast detection (Cao et al., 2020).

Carbon dots (CDs) are a diverse group of small carbon-based nanoparticles (<10 nm) with varying shapes, crystallinity, and photophysical properties. They include graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbide polymer dots (CPDs) (Ðorđević et al., 2022). CDs stand out for their excellent optical properties, environmental friendliness, wide range of raw materials, low cost, good biocompatibility, and other advantages. Among these, doped CDs prepared through bottom-up syntheses offer versatility, with the selection of a suitable carbon source containing various functional groups being a vital factor for producing desired functional doped CDs. Glutathione (GSH), known for its abundant functional groups (–COOH, -SH, –NH2, –NH-, and –CONH-), besides being a carbon source, is an ideal material for the preparation of doped CDs. CDs exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity in analytical and optical sensing fields, making them ideal for designing sensors. In recent years, there has been significant interest in the development of ratiometric fluorescent sensors with dual-signal response based on carbon dots (CDs) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) due to their self-calibration properties (Yuan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). This is primarily attributed to the fact that OPD can be easily oxidized to 2,3-diaminophenothiazine (DAP) under the catalysis of analytes, resulting in a new fluorescence emission at 560 nm. The fluorescence intensity of DAP increases with the concentration of analytes, while that of CDs decreases. For instance, Yuan et al. fabricated a ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal method for analyzing H2O2 and uric acid using CQDs/OPDs (Yuan et al., 2021). Similarly, Zhang et al. developed a sensing platform of CDs/OPDs for the identification and detection of penicillamine (Zhang et al., 2022).

In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using GSH. The prepared N-CNDs and OPD were utilized to construct fluorometric and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platforms for the detection of Cr(VI)/Ag(I) and HCHO, respectively.

The materials used in this study included OPD, GSH (Aladdin, Shanghai, China), HCHO, glutaraldehyde, benzaldehyde, acetaldehyde (Tianjin Komeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), deionized ultrapure water, and sodium citrate-citrate buffer (0.1 M, pH 3.0–5.8). Metal ion solutions were prepared from high-purity AgNO3 and K2Cr2O7. All chemicals used were either analytical or high purity without requiring pretreatment.

The instruments utilized in this work included an X-ray powders diffractometer (XRD) (Zeiss Merlin, Germany), an X-ray electron spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Fisher K-Alpha, America), a small autoclave (Fuzhou Keleishi Test Equipment Co., Ltd.), a blast drying oven (Boxun Technology Co., Ltd.), an F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi Limited, Japan), a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi Limited, Japan), a Nicolet 5700 infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Nicolet, America), an FLS 980 time-resolved spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain), an FEI field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 F20, America), and a Zeta potentiometer (ZS3600 Malvern, Britain). The pH values of the aqueous solutions were adjusted using a PB-10 exact digital pH meter (Sartorius, Germany).

To prepare the N-CNDs, 0.25 g of GSH was dissolved in 5 mL of H2O in a 25 mL autoclave. The mixture was then sonicated and reacted in a blast dryer at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling the reactor to room temperature, the filtrate was collected through a 0.22 μm filter and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CNDs/OPD ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal detection of Ag(I) and Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To detect Ag(I) and Cr(VI), a total sample volume of 3.0 mL was prepared by mixing 600 μL of 50 mM OPD with various concentrations of Ag(I)/Cr(VI), followed by the addition of 30 μL of N-CNDs (8.13 mM), 625 μL of 0.1 M citric acid-sodium citrate buffer at pH 5.4, and H2O in sequential order. After thorough mixing using a vortex mixer and an 8-minute incubation away from light, the resulting samples were subjected to fluorescence ratio measurement (F 560/F 430) under excitation at 360 nm and absorbance measurement at 432 nm (A 432).

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal detection of HCHO
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of HCHO, a total sample volume of 3.0 mL was prepared by mixing a series of HCHO solutions with 300 μL of 120 μg mL−1 Cr(VI), 600 μL of 50 mM OPD, 30 μL of N-CNDs (8.13 mM), 625 μL of 0.1 M citric acid-sodium citrate buffer at pH 5.4, and H2O. After an 8-minute incubation away from light, the resulting samples were subjected to fluorescence ratio measurement (F 430/F 560) under excitation at 360 nm and absorbance measurement at 432 nm (A 432).

Water samples: Environmental water samples from Qingshan Lake in Huangshi, Hubei Province, China, were filtered using a 0.22 μm filtering membrane. The collected filter liquor was stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

Red wine samples: An appropriate amount of red wine purchased from local supermarkets was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane, and the filter liquor was collected and stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

Frozen chicken and shrimp samples: Accurate weight of 2.0 g of frozen chicken and shrimp purchased from local supermarkets was extracted with 4.0 mL of ultrapure water for 5 h. After centrifugation to remove residual precipitation, the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. The filter liquor was collected and stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CNDs/OPD ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal detection of Ag(I) and Cr(VI) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Ag(I) and Cr(VI) in real samples, a series of 500 μL prepared real sample solutions spiked with different concentrations of Ag(I)/Cr(VI) (Ag(I): 0.0, 6.0, 80 and 160 μM for ratio fluorescence; 0.0, 10, 100 and 200 μM for colorimetric; Cr(VI): 0.0, 58, 154 and 231 μM for ratio fluorescence; 0.0, 19, 173 and 346 μM for colorimetric, respectively), were mixed with 600 μL of 50 mM OPD, 30 μL of N-CNDs (8.13 mM), 625 μL of 0.1 M citric acid-sodium citrate buffer at pH 5.4, masking reagents (100 μM KCl for Ag(I) in probing Cr(VI); 1 mM H2O2 for Cr(VI) in analysis of Ag(I)), and H2O in sequential order. The final sample volume was fixed at 3.0 mL. After thorough mixing using a vortex mixer and an 8-minute incubation away from light, the resulting samples were subjected to fluorescence ratio measurement (F 560/F 430) under excitation at 360 nm and absorbance measurement at 432 nm (A 432).

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal detection of HCHO in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: A series of 500 μL real sample solutions were prepared and spiked with various concentrations of formaldehyde (0.0, 3.0, 40, and 100 μM for ratio fluorescence; 0.0, 4.0, 60, and 180 μM for colorimetric). These solutions were mixed well with 300 μL of 120 μg mL−1 Cr(VI), 600 μL of 50 mM OPD, 30 μL of N-CNDs (8.13 mM), 625 μL of 0.1 M citric acid-sodium citrate buffer at pH 5.4, and H2O to a total volume of 3.0 mL. After being kept away from light for 8 min, the fluorescence ratio (F 430/F 560) was measured under excitation at 360 nm, and the absorbance at 432 nm (A 432) of the resulting samples was also determined.

GSH was used as the carbon source, and the effect of GSH dosage (0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25 g) on the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs was investigated. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs did not significantly change with increasing GSH dosage (Fig. S1 (a)), and there was no noticeable blue-shift or red-shift in the emission wavelengths of N-CNDs (Fig. S1 (b)). This indicates that the effect of glutathione dosage is minimal, and 0.25 g of GSH was chosen as the optimal dosage.

The reason why 0.25 g of GSH was sufficient for N-CNDs preparation is as follows: the saturation solubility of GSH is 50 mg mL−1, and as mentioned in Section 2.3 Preparation of N-CNDs, 0.25 g of GSH in 5 mL of water corresponds to a concentration of (0.25 × 1000 mg)/5 mL = 50 mg mL−1. Therefore, when GSH was increased from 0.25 g to 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, and 1.25 g, the concentration of the saturated solution of GSH remained the same, resulting in no significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs (Fig. S1 (a)).

In the one-step hydrothermal method, the synthesis temperature and time are important factors affecting the material's performance. Therefore, the effects of reaction temperature (160, 180, 200 ℃) and time (2, 4, 6, 8 h) on the quantum yields were investigated. The results in Table S1 show that, at the same reaction time, higher reaction temperatures and longer reaction times led to higher quantum yields of the product for temperatures of 160 and 180 ℃. However, at a temperature of 200 ℃, the optimum quantum yield reached 21.2%.

In summary, the optimal synthesis conditions for N-CNDs were determined as follows: 0.25 g of GSH, 5 mL of H2O, 4 h of reaction time, and a reaction temperature of 200 °C. The concentration of N-CNDs was 0.813 M (Boonta et al., 2020).

N-CNDs were characterized using techniques such as TEM, zeta potential analysis, FTIR, and XPS. TEM analysis (Fig. 1 (a)) reveals the morphology of N-CNDs, showing homogeneous, lattice-free, spherical carbon particles with an average size of 2.5 ± 0.5 nm (inset of Fig. 1(a)). XRD diffractogram (Fig. 1(b)) displays two broad peaks at 20.6° and 22.3°, corresponding to the (002) plane and indicating poor crystallinity (Arumugam and Kim, 2018). Zeta potential analysis (Fig. 1(c)) showed a value of 8.01 mV, indicating a positive charge on the surface of N-CNDs. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 1(d)) reveals characteristic peaks at 3400, 1733, and 1227 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of NH/OH, CO, and COC, respectively. Peaks at 1641 and 1391 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibration of NH in –CO-NH- and CN, respectively (Qu et al., 2020a; Das and Dutta, 2021). The absence of the characteristic peak of -SH near 2050 cm−1 from GSH in the N-CNDs confirms its decomposition during the reaction (Du et al., 2014).

The chemical composition of N-CNDs was determined using XPS analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2(a)) reveals peaks at 531.36, 400.05, and 285.3 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. High-resolution spectra (Fig. 2(b), (c), (d)) identify peaks associated with various functional groups such as CC/CC (284.8 eV), CN (286.05, 399.5 eV), CO (287.8 eV), –COOH (288.65 eV), COC/C-OH (532.7 eV), CO (531.3 eV), and NH (401.5 eV) (Du et al., 2014; Song et al., 2016; Niu et al., 2016; Jiao et al., 2019).

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-CNDs are presented in Fig. S2. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were determined to be 360 nm and 430 nm, respectively. The broad UV absorption spectra ranged from 300 nm to 400 nm, with a maximum absorption peak at 294 nm. This is attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds and the n-π* transition of CO/CN bonds (Jiao et al., 2019; Mehta et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis route of N-CNDs and construction mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal Cr(VI), Ag(I) by N-CNDs/OPD and HCHO by N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 illustrates the synthesis route of N-CNDs and the construction mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal detection of Cr(VI), Ag(I) using N-CNDs/OPD, and HCHO using N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI). As observed, N-CNDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using GSH. Under the catalysis of Cr(VI)/Ag(I), OPD underwent oxidation to form DAP, resulting in a new fluorescence emission peak at 560 nm. The fluorescence intensity of DAP increased with an increase in the concentration of Cr(VI)/Ag(I), along with a corresponding enhancement in the optical colorimetric absorbance at 432 nm. Concurrently, the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs decreased due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect (FRET) between DAP and N-CNDs.

Based on the reaction 3HCHO + 2Cr2O7 2−+16H+=4Cr(III) + 3CO2 + 11H2O, HCHO effectively inhibited the catalytic reaction between Cr(VI) and OPD under weakly acidic conditions. This led to a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 560 nm and absorbance at 432 nm, accompanied by an increase in fluorescence intensity at 430 nm. Hence, dual-readout sensing platforms utilizing ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry were fabricated for the detection of Cr(VI)/Ag(I) using N-CNDs/OPD and HCHO using N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal Cr(VI) and Ag(I) sensing platform based on N-CNDs/OPD

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effects of pH on the systems involving N-CNDs, N-CNDs/OPD/Ag(I), and N-CNDs/OPD/Ag(I) with and without HCHO, 0.1 M citric acid-sodium citrate buffer at different pH values (3.0, 3.4, 3.8, 4.2, 4.6, 5.0, 5.4, 5.8) was utilized. As shown in Fig. S3 (a), the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs at 430 nm remained relatively constant across different pH values. Fig. S3(b) illustrates the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs/OPD/Ag(I) at 430 nm and 560 nm. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm exhibited minimal variation, while at 560 nm, it showed an increasing trend with increasing pH from 3.0 to 5.8. This could be attributed to the protonation of the amino group of OPD under weakly acidic conditions, leading to the inhibition of the oxidation reaction between Ag(I) and OPD (Zhao et al., 2017). By comparing the differences in fluorescence intensity at 430 nm and 560 nm, it was determined that a suitable pH value for N-CNDs/OPD/Ag(I) must be above 5.0. Fig. S3(c) demonstrates the effect of pH on ΔF 430 and ΔF 560 of N-CNDs/OPD/Ag(I) and N-CNDs/OPD/Ag(I)/HCHO, respectively. Notably, the minimum difference between ΔF 430 and ΔF 560 was observed at pH 5.4. Taking into account the comprehensive analysis of the aforementioned results and the subsequent sensitivity in HCHO detection, pH 5.4 was selected as the optimal pH.

Fig. 3 demonstrates that the presence of Cr(VI)/Ag(I) catalyst results in the oxidation of OPD to DAP. Consequently, there is an increase in the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 560 nm, the optical colorimetric absorbance of DAP at 432 nm, and a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs at 430 nm. This indicates that the sensitivity of the N-CNDs/OPD sensor for detecting Cr(VI) and Ag(I) is directly proportional to the amount of DAP produced. As the concentration of OPD directly affects the total amount of DAP formed, it plays a crucial role in determining the detection sensitivity of Cr(VI)/Ag(I). Therefore, the effect of OPD concentration on the fluorescence intensity of F 560/F 430 was investigated. The results presented in Fig. S4 demonstrate a significant increase in F 560/F 430 with increasing OPD concentration from 0 to 8 mM, followed by a stable plateau from 8 to 10 mM. Consequently, an OPD concentration of 10 mM was selected as the optimum.

Considering the need for rapid testing, we examined the effect of response time on N-CNDs/OPD sensing platform for detecting Ag(I) and Cr(VI) separately. The results displayed in Fig. S5 indicate that F 560/F 430 increases rapidly within the first 6 min for both Ag(I) and Cr(VI), after which it reaches a plateau with minimal changes. Consequently, the optimal response time was determined to be 8 min, indicating the fast response characteristics of the method.

Under optimal conditions, the effects of various metal ions such as K(I), Ca(II), Mg(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Mn(II), Ba(II), Zr(IV), Cd(II), Cu(II), Ag(I), Fe(II), and Fe(III) on the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs/OPD were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), it is evident that, except for Cr(VI) and Ag(I), the other ions have negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs/OPD. This confirms the excellent selectivity of N-CNDs/OPD towards Cr(VI) and Ag(I) and its robust anti-interference capability.

To ensure accurate and exclusive determination of Cr(VI) or Ag(I), masking agents such as H2O2 and KCl were employed to eliminate interference from Cr(VI) during Ag(I) analysis and vice versa. Based on the reactions 3H2O2 + Cr2O7 2−+8H+= 2Cr3++ 3O2 + 7H2O and Ag++Cl−=AgCl (precipitate), the results depicted in Fig. 4(b) and 4(c) demonstrate the satisfactory masking effect achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal HCHO sensing platform based on N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI)
Effect of time and Cr(VI) concentration
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve optimal sensitivity and reduced reaction time for HCHO analysis, the effects of reaction time and Cr(VI) concentration on the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) were investigated. As shown in Fig. S6(a), F430/F 560 gradually increases with response time within 6 min, reaching a stable plateau thereafter. Therefore, an optical time of 8 min was chosen. Fig. S6(b) reveals that △F 430/△F 560 increases with increasing Cr(VI) concentration from 25 to 135 μM, reaching a maximum at 135 μM, but decreases with further increases in Cr(VI) concentration. Based on the redox reaction 3HCHO + 2Cr2O7 2−+16H+=4Cr(III) + 3CO2 + 11H2O, this may be due to insufficient reaction at lower Cr(VI) concentrations (<135 μM), while excessive Cr(VI) limits the ability of HCHO to inhibit the catalytic oxidation of OPD to DAP by Cr(VI), resulting in a decrease in △F 430/△F 560. Hence, 135 μM Cr(VI) was determined as the optimum concentration.

To assess the specificity of the N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) sensing platform for HCHO detection, responses between N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) and other aldehydes (glutaraldehyde, benzaldehyde, acetaldehyde), as well as potential co-existing substances in real samples (glutathione, phenol, Fe(III), glucose) were investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, except for HCHO, N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) exhibited minimal response to other substances, demonstrating its excellent selectivity towards HCHO. This can be attributed to the redox reaction between HCHO and Cr(VI) under acidic conditions (3HCHO + 2Cr2O7 2−+16H+=4Cr(III) + 3CO2 + 11H2O), which effectively inhibits the catalytic oxidation of OPD to DAP by Cr(VI). Furthermore, under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, only the presence of HCHO could restore the blue fluorescence of N-CNDs, further confirming the exceptional selectivity of N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) for HCHO and its suitability for analyzing HCHO in complex matrix samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal probing Ag(I)/Cr(VI) by N-CNDs/OPD and HCHO by N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(Ⅵ)
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the mechanism underlying the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal detection of Ag(I) and Cr(VI) by N-CNDs/OPD and HCHO by N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(Ⅵ), we selected Cr(VI) as the representative ion and examined the fluorescence emission spectra and UV absorption spectra of different systems composed of N-CNDs, OPD, Cr(VI), and HCHO. As shown in Fig. S8, while N-CNDs/Cr(VI) and N-CNDs/HCHO exhibit some fluorescence quenching and enhancement effects at 430 nm, respectively, N-CNDs/OPD remains relatively unchanged. However, when Cr(VI) is added to N-CNDs/OPD, there is a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity at 430 nm, accompanied by the appearance of a new fluorescence emission peak at 560 nm (Fig. S8(a)). Additionally, a new UV absorption peak emerges at 432 nm (Fig. S8(b)), demonstrating a similar phenomenon in the case of OPD/Cr(VI). This can be attributed to the catalytic oxidation of OPD by Cr(VI), resulting in the generation of DAP, which exhibits an emission wavelength at 560 nm and a UV absorption peak at 432 nm.

In Fig. S8(c), there is a significant overlap between the absorption peak of DAP at 432 nm and the excitation spectrum of N-CNDs (λex = 360 nm), as well as a nearly complete overlap with its emission spectrum (λem = 430 nm). This suggests the presence of internal filtration effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between N-CNDs and DAP, leading to a pronounced fluorescence decrease of N-CNDs. On the other hand, in the presence of HCHO, the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) increases at 430 nm and decreases at 560 nm (Fig. S8(a)), accompanied by a decrease in absorbance at 432 nm (Fig. S8(b)). This is attributed to the redox reaction between HCHO and Cr(VI) under acidic conditions (3HCHO + 2Cr2O7 2−+16H+=4Cr(III) + 3CO2 + 11H2O), which inhibits the transformation of OPD to DAP due to the consumption of Cr(VI).

Fluorescence lifetime is considered an effective factor for distinguishing IFE from FRET. The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CNDs with and without DAP were measured separately. As shown in Fig. S8(d), with the addition of DAP, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CNDs decreases from 7.43 to 6.01 ns, indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism between DAP and N-CNDs is mainly attributed to FRET rather than IFE.

Previous studies have reported that HCHO does not directly react with DAP (Deng et al., 2020), while Cr(VI) and OPD may undergo reactions with HCHO (Zuo et al., 2022). To further investigate whether OPD or Cr(VI) is responsible for the fluorescence decrease at 560 nm in N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) in the presence of HCHO, we studied the effects of OPD and Cr(VI) concentrations on F 430/F 560 in the presence and absence of HCHO, respectively. In Fig. S9 (a), the fluorescence signal of F 430/F 560 is not observed until the concentration of Cr(VI) reaches 96 μM in the presence of 100 μM HCHO and 10 mM OPD. After that, the fluorescence signal shows a parallel increase in both N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) and N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI)/HCHO systems. This indicates that the inhibition of N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) by HCHO is mostly saturated by an equivalent amount of Cr(VI). In Fig. S9 (b), without HCHO, the F 430/F 560 ratio increases with increasing OPD content from 0 to 8 mM, and then reaches a stable plateau at 8–12 mM. However, in the presence of HCHO, the F 430/F 560 ratio shows little variation with OPD concentration from 0 to 12 mM. These results reveal that the main factor causing the weakening of the fluorescence signal at 560 nm is the redox interaction between HCHO and Cr(VI), rather than the Schiff base reaction of HCHO and OPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of a new N-CNDs/OPD-based ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal method for the detection of Ag(I), Cr(VI) and HCHO sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the N-CNDs/OPD-based ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal method for the detection of Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO was investigated under optimal conditions. Linearity, precision, and limit of detection (LOD) experiments were performed and the results are summarized in Table S2 and Fig. 5 . The dual-signal and visible mode of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection show good linearity with acceptable regression coefficients (R2) ranging from 0.9901 to 0.9981. The linear ranges are as follows: ratiometric fluorescence −1.0–180 μM for Ag(I), 2.9–288 μM for Cr(VI), and 0.5–120 μM for HCHO; colorimetry − 1.0–200 μM for Ag(I), 1.0–404 μM for Cr(VI), and 1.0–200 μM for HCHO. The LODs are low, with values of 0.42, 0.54, and 0.29 μM for Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO, respectively, in the ratiometric fluorescence mode, and 0.58, 0.58, and 0.34 μM for Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO, respectively, in the colorimetric mode.

Table S3 compares the analytical performance of the present method with other literature methods for the detection of Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO. The present method demonstrates some advantages in terms of LODs and linear ranges. For Ag(I), the LOD is much lower than reference (Arumugam and Kim, 2018), and similar to some works (Liao et al., 2018; May et al., 2022), but higher than another reference (Gong et al., 2016). The linear range is wider compared to some references (Gong et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022). Furthermore, this work achieves dual-signal and visible detection mode of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry by simply adding OPD, which is simpler and cheaper compared to our previous work (Gong et al., 2016). For Cr(VI), the LOD is lower compared to some works (Pacquiao et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2022; Sapyen et al., 2022), but higher than others (He et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2019), and the linear range is the widest among the listed reports. For HCHO, the LOD is lower compared to some studies (Wang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2017; Qu et al., 2020b), but higher than others, and the linear range is broader compared to some reports (Tu et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021), and the linear range is broader than reports (Wang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2017; Tu et al., 2020；Zhao et al., 2021). Additionally, the method demonstrates excellent selectivity, wide linear range, and fast response speed for the analysis of Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO using the dual-signal and visible detection mode of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry, providing richer, more reliable, convenient, and accurate data.

To validate the accuracy and applicability of the developed method, real samples including water, red wine, frozen shrimp/chicken, were analyzed for Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO using the established method under optimal conditions (Table S2). The results presented in Table 1 demonstrate recoveries ranging from 93.6% to 110.9% with relative standard deviations (RSD) below 2.9%. These findings indicate acceptable recoveries and the practical potential of the method for detecting Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO in complex sample matrices.

In this study, N-CNDs with a quantum yield of 21.2% were synthesized using a simple and environmentally friendly approach. Ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platforms were developed for the detection of Cr(VI) by N-CNDs/OPD and Ag(I) by N-CNDs/OPD, respectively. Additionally, HCHO detection was achieved using N-CNDs/OPD/Cr(VI) based on the redox interaction between HCHO and Cr(VI) and the FRET mechanism between DAP and N-CNDs. This study offers the following advantages: (1) Simple and environmentally friendly synthesis of N-CNDs with excellent optical and chemical stability; (2) Dual-signal sensing modes of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection, providing richer, more reliable, convenient, and accurate results with wider adaptability; (3) The developed sensing platforms exhibit fast response speed, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, and strong anti-interference abilities, making them highly suitable for the detection of Ag(I), Cr(VI), and HCHO in complex sample matrices.

Wenwen Guo: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yaya Lei: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiaoxiao Yu: Investigation, Resources. Yiwei Wu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province of China (2022CFD038) and Educational Commission of Hubei Province of China (B2021129).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137945.
